metadata.summary: Patterns for getting parseable JSON from LLMs: JSON Mode, Structured Outputs, Claude retry loop, and function-forcing. Use Pydantic with Field descriptions.

content.title: Structured Output Basics
content.summary: Patterns for getting LLMs to return consistent, parseable JSON: OpenAI JSON Mode (response_format), OpenAI Structured Outputs (beta.parse with Pydantic), Claude prompt + json.loads + retry loop, and function-based forcing via tool calling. The core rule: define schemas in Pydantic with Field(description=...) on every field — descriptions become part of the JSON Schema and measurably improve model accuracy.

why.p: LLM outputs integrated into applications and APIs must be parseable. JSON Mode guarantees valid JSON but not schema compliance — production pipelines break when required fields are absent or typed wrong. OpenAI Structured Outputs (beta.parse) gives schema-level guarantees for gpt-4o; Claude requires a prompt + retry loop. Without Pydantic and Field descriptions, the model guesses field semantics and produces semantically invalid data even when JSON is structurally valid.

list.item: Any agent pipeline step that must pass typed data to a downstream system (DB write, API call, UI render)
Data extraction from unstructured text (invoices, forms, articles, emails)
Classification tasks where the output enum must be validated
When Pydantic models already exist for the domain — reuse them as response schemas
Replacing regex-based parsing of LLM output with schema-enforced extraction
Simple yes/no or short free-text responses where schema overhead adds latency without value
Tasks requiring narrative output (copywriting, explanations, debugging commentary)
When beta.parse is unavailable for the target model — use the function-forcing pattern instead
When the schema changes frequently at runtime — static Pydantic models are hard to generate dynamically

rule.statement: Even with Structured Outputs, validate the parsed result before passing to the next pipeline step — Structured Outputs enforce structure but not semantic correctness; a required string field may contain nonsense.
rule.rationale: Structural validity (correct JSON shape) does not imply semantic validity (correct values). Downstream steps must validate business rules independently of the schema.

list.item: JSON Mode (OpenAI, Anthropic): guarantees valid JSON — NOT schema compliance. External validation required.
Structured Outputs (OpenAI beta.parse, gpt-4o only): guarantees valid JSON AND schema compliance
Prompt engineering (all providers): most flexible, least reliable — always add retry logic
Function-forcing pattern (all providers): reliable across all OpenAI models; works on Claude too

example.code:
from openai import OpenAI import json
client = OpenAI()
response = client.chat.completions.create( model="gpt-4o", messages=[ {"role": "system", "content": 'Extract entities. Return JSON: {"entities": [{"name": str, "type": str}]}'}, {"role": "user", "content": "Apple CEO Tim Cook announced a new iPhone at Cupertino."} ], response_format={"type": "json_object"} ) result = json.loads(response.choices[0].message.content) # Always validate: result may have valid JSON but wrong shape assert "entities" in result, "Missing entities key"

antipattern.description: Passing JSON Mode output to downstream code without schema validation.
antipattern.reason: JSON Mode guarantees parseable JSON, not that required keys exist or values are the right type. Missing keys cause KeyError in the consumer.

section.name: Pydantic schema definition

rule.statement: Add Field(description=...) to every Pydantic field — descriptions become part of the JSON Schema sent to the model and measurably improve accuracy on ambiguous fields.
rule.rationale: The model uses field descriptions to understand semantics. Without descriptions, it infers meaning from the field name alone, which fails for abbreviated or domain-specific names.

section.reference: Pydantic models for entity extraction and invoice parsing

example.code:
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()
response = client.beta.chat.completions.parse( model="gpt-4o", messages=[ {"role": "system", "content": "Extract entities and provide a summary."}, {"role": "user", "content": "Elon Musk visited Berlin to open a new Tesla factory."} ], response_format=ExtractionResult )
result: ExtractionResult = response.choices[0].message.parsed # result is None if model refuses (content policy) — always check if result is None: raise ValueError("Model refused to generate structured output")
print(result.entities[0].name) # "Elon Musk" print(result.summary)
example.note: beta.parse is gpt-4o family only. Not available for o1/o3 reasoning models.

rule.statement: Check response.choices[0].message.parsed for None before accessing — it is None when the model refuses due to content policy.
rule.rationale: Accessing .name on None raises AttributeError; this error surfaces in the consumer far from the LLM call, making it hard to diagnose.

section.name: Claude JSON Extraction with Retry Loop
section.p: Claude lacks native Structured Outputs; use prompt + markdown-fence stripping + json.loads + retry with a corrective message on parse failure.

rule.statement: Always strip markdown code fences before parsing Claude JSON output — Claude sometimes wraps JSON in triple-backtick blocks even when told not to.
rule.rationale: A prompt saying "return only JSON" reduces but does not eliminate markdown fences; the strip must always be present or json.loads raises a JSONDecodeError.

section.reference: Claude structured extraction with retry loop

section.name: Complex Schemas: Tasks, Invoices, Enums
section.p: Pydantic models for nested structured extraction: task with subtasks, invoice with line items, enums for fixed-vocabulary fields.

rule.statement: Keep schemas flat (2 levels of nesting max) for highest reliability; deeply nested schemas degrade model accuracy even with Structured Outputs.
rule.rationale: Model accuracy on deeply nested JSON drops as nesting depth increases; this is empirical, not a hard API constraint.

rule.statement: Use Literal types or enums for fixed-vocabulary fields instead of free strings — this removes ambiguity and makes downstream handling exhaustive.
rule.rationale: A string field "priority" can return "High", "high", "HIGH", or "urgent"; an enum returns only the declared values.

section.reference: Task and invoice extraction Pydantic models

example.code:
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field from typing import List, Optional from enum import Enum
class Priority(str, Enum): LOW = "low" MEDIUM = "medium" HIGH = "high" CRITICAL = "critical"
class Subtask(BaseModel): title: str completed: bool = False
class Task(BaseModel): title: str = Field(..., min_length=1, max_length=200) description: Optional[str] = None priority: Priority = Priority.MEDIUM tags: List[str] = [] subtasks: List[Subtask] = [] estimated_hours: Optional[float] = Field(None, ge=0)
class ProjectExtraction(BaseModel): project_name: str tasks: List[Task] team_members: List[str]

class InvoiceItem(BaseModel): description: str quantity: float unit_price: float total: float
class Invoice(BaseModel): invoice_number: str date: str vendor_name: str customer_name: str items: List[InvoiceItem] subtotal: float tax: Optional[float] = None total: float currency: str = "USD"
example.note: Use Optional for fields that may be absent in real documents; avoids validation failures on partial extractions.

section.name: Function-forcing pattern
section.p: Force structured output via function calling — works on any OpenAI model. Alternatively, use the tool-forcing pattern for Claude.

checklist.check: Define schemas in Pydantic, not raw dicts — IDE type-checking, .model_validate, and .model_json_schema() come for free
Use Field(description=...) on every field — descriptions become part of the JSON Schema and improve model accuracy
Prefer Optional[str] over str for fields that may be absent — forces model to handle missing data explicitly
Use enums (Literal[...]) instead of free strings for fixed-vocabulary fields
Always validate the parsed result before passing to the next pipeline step — even Structured Outputs can produce semantically wrong data
Keep schemas flat (2 levels max) for highest reliability; deeply nested schemas degrade accuracy
Log the raw LLM text alongside the parsed model for debugging — parsing errors are easier to diagnose with raw output

template.purpose: Pydantic models for entity extraction and invoice parsing with Field descriptions
Claude structured extraction with retry loop and markdown-fence stripping

section.name: Agentic workflow
section.p: Agents use Pydantic models as the canonical contract between pipeline stages: the model is defined once and used both as the LLM response schema and as the type annotation for the next step's input. For OpenAI, client.beta.chat.completions.parse eliminates the json.loads + validation step. For Claude, wrap the call in a retry loop that sends a corrective message if parsing fails. Always log both the raw text and the parsed result for debugging.
Recommended subagents: faion-sdd-executor-agent (uses Pydantic models to enforce spec/design/task output shapes); nero-sdd-executor-agent (same for NERO pipelines; task outputs typed as dataclasses).

antipattern.description: JSON Mode (OpenAI, Anthropic) guarantees valid JSON but NOT schema compliance — downstream validation is still required
antipattern.reason: Valid JSON does not mean required keys are present or values match the schema.

antipattern.description: OpenAI Structured Outputs (beta.parse) is gpt-4o family only; not available for o1/o3 reasoning models
antipattern.reason: Fall back to function-forcing pattern for unsupported models.

antipattern.description: Claude has no native Structured Outputs equivalent — must use prompt engineering + json.loads with retry
antipattern.reason: Claude has tool use but no parsed response type like OpenAI.

antipattern.description: Deeply nested schemas with many optional fields produce inconsistent results even with Structured Outputs
antipattern.reason: Model accuracy degrades with nesting depth.

antipattern.description: Very large schemas (>50 fields) increase prompt token cost and reduce model accuracy
antipattern.reason: Context and model capacity are both limited.

antipattern.description: Pydantic validators run client-side after parsing — API errors and validation errors need separate handling
antipattern.reason: Schema constraints like ge=0 are not enforced by the LLM itself.

antipattern.description: response.choices[0].message.parsed is None when the model refuses to generate structured output (content policy)
antipattern.reason: Always check for None before accessing.

antipattern.description: OpenAI Structured Outputs are not supported for streaming + parse simultaneously (as of 2026)
antipattern.reason: Choose one — either streaming OR parse.

antipattern.description: Claude sometimes wraps JSON in ```json ``` even when told not to — always strip markdown code fences before parsing
antipattern.reason: Markdown wrapping is not fully controlled by prompts.

antipattern.description: instructor library adds retry overhead but can mask model failures; set a max retry limit and surface errors explicitly
antipattern.reason: Retries can hide the real problem when limits are too high.

antipattern.description: Large schemas (>20 fields) increase prompt tokens significantly — measure token cost before shipping complex extraction pipelines
antipattern.reason: Token cost scales with schema complexity.

rule.statement: Define schemas in Pydantic, not raw dicts — IDE type-checking, .model_validate, and .model_json_schema() come for free.
rule.rationale: Pydantic integration eliminates boilerplate validation and provides runtime safety.

rule.statement: Prefer Optional[str] over str for fields that may be absent — forces model to handle missing data explicitly.
rule.rationale: Optional fields allow the model to omit them; required fields force a value or validation failure.

rule.statement: Always validate the parsed result before passing to the next pipeline step — even Structured Outputs can produce semantically wrong data.
rule.rationale: Structural validity is not semantic validity.
